title: Unions, industry super oppose strict 12-year terms: APRA faces battle over board limits
full_text:
The prudential regulator will put in place tough tenure limits for directors at some of the country's largest financial institutions despite criticism from companies and unions that say the watchdog has overstepped its brief and provided little evidence to justify the revamped rules.
Australian Prudential Regulation Authority chairman John Lonsdale said its controversial governance reforms "sought to strike the right balance between safety and efficiency" for banks, insurers and super funds.
But a decision to stick with a strict 12-year limit on board director terms clashes with feedback from these industries, which largely derided the attempt to impose a cap in submissions to the process released on Tuesday.
The changes mark APRA's most significant overhaul of its governance rules in more than a decade. They were announced after a string of scandals, including allegations of market manipulation at ANZ and the CFMEU's control over retirement savings fund Cbus in 2024 and 2025.
While the watchdog has walked back some of its most contested proposals - including what was effectively a veto power over who is appointed to boards - and extended the potential limit on board terms from 10 years to 12 years, the submissions show APRA faces a challenge to win the sector's support for the new rules, due to come into effect in 2028.
Opposition to tenure limits was particularly fierce from the major industry super funds and the unions, which, like employer groups, appoint directors to those funds under a long-standing equal-representation model.
The Australian Council of Trade Unions said the changes were "inconsistent with best practice regulatory principles and demonstrate concerning regulatory overreach and insufficient consideration of member outcomes".
In a submission that also accused APRA of overstepping parliament with the change, the ACTU said "there is no evidence to suggest that tenure limits improve governance practices, member experience or fund outcomes".
"In contrast, the most highly functioning boards blend the stability, expertise and experience of long-standing directors with the integration and benefit of newer directors with less fund-specific experience," it said.
The Australian Services Union categorised the proposals as an "ill-informed attack" on trade union-appointed board directors, while AustralianSuper - the nation's largest super fund managing more than $400 billion in assets - and chief strategy officer Paula Benson said there was "no justification for prescriptive time limits" as local industry funds outperformed global peers.
"[APRA] ignores the outstanding performance of Australia's superannuation system when compared with overseas countries where the prudential regulator has a veto power," Benson said in her submission.
"The argument in the paper also ignores APRA's existing power ... to apply to the Federal Court to remove a director," she said.
CareSuper, which has about $60 billion under management, said tenure limits may "disrupt the continuity and experience of representative boards".
The unions and funds were joined by the Australian Institute of Company Directors, which described APRA's move as a "rigid proposal [that] does not appropriately reflect the benefits that come from long-serving directors who have developed a deep understanding of an entity's complex operation".
CGI Glass Lewis, an influential governance advisory firm used by institutional investors, said that although 10 years was a reasonable limit for a director to stay at a single company, a cap could affect succession planning.
"There is a risk that capable non-executive directors already partway through a ... tenure may be overlooked for the chair role," it said.
APRA, however, said a hard limit was required to ensure board renewal.
"A hard limit is necessary to consistently strengthen renewal discipline and reduce the risk of entrenchment over time," its paper said.
Lonsdale said strong governance standards were fundamental to ensuring the financial system remained resilient. "Over a long period of time, APRA has observed that problems at our regulated entities can be frequently traced to poor oversight, unclear accountability or weak challenge," he said.
"At a time of rising economic and geopolitical uncertainty, and where new technologies are rapidly changing financial services, our regulated entities need leaders who can respond decisively and effectively to financial stress and operational disruptions."
APRA will now embark on another round of consultation before finalising the standards later this year, applying them in January 2028.
Draft rules circulated on Tuesday said boards could still approve a 12-month extension to a director's 12-year tenure as long as they notify the regulator.
The term limit proposal did find supporters in the Australian Shareholders Association and retail customers who made submissions. Australian Retirement Trust, the country's second-largest retirement savings fund manager, said it had already imposed a 12-year limit on directors, but requested the regulator consider "some flexibility where the enforcement of a hard limit could lead to an undesirable outcome".
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: Lucas Baird
heading: Regulation
